So memory learning is, if you go through the cog neuro literature, or even the neuroscience literature, it's the most popular topic, it's going to dominate, you know, 50% of all the articles.

And so clear interest in studying processes underlying memory and learning.

Well, of course, question that really was central to many, many studies for, you know, really, for lots of years of memory research is where is memory, right?

We knew that, you know, if we looked at the brain, we could see that back of the brain was clearly important for vision, you could tell that from people had brain injury, round here was important for motor control that made people think that, you know, memory is probably stored in a particular place in the brain.

And that was sort of the notion that there was an engram, engram is kind of a word for like, here's this consolidated, like sort of storage of memory that somehow is packed away in our brain, kind of the way you might think about with a computer that we might file away the information or maybe in a desk drawer.
And there's been actually, you know, that search for the memory.
There's actually a very long history in neuroscience where the idea was, let's do lesion studies, can we actually destroy parts of the brain and wipe out selective memories?

We knew we could destroy parts of the brain and you know, people would have trouble retrieving information or not people, different species, but could we really show that it was really a problem with memory per se, rather than say, performance and so forth.
There's been work done in fruit flies and rabbits, all sorts of species here developed optogenetics or genetic knockouts there.

And there has been progress made in understanding where our memories, but much of that work has really, you know, come to the idea that, you know, memory isn't probably in a single location, but rather is actually a network of information.
